title: The Problem Isn't Prompts. It's Context.
excerpt: I built my company with AI and never wrote a clever prompt. Here's what mattered instead.
content:
Every day I see people obsessing over prompts. They collect prompt libraries. They save templates. They spend twenty minutes rewriting a prompt because the last answer wasn't quite right. Then they conclude the AI isn't very good. I think they're solving the wrong problem.

Imagine hiring an employee and re-explaining your business to them every morning. Who your customers are. What your company does. How you write. What good work looks like. What mistakes to avoid. Every single day. Nobody would work that way.

Yet that's exactly how most people use AI. They start a fresh conversation and expect the system to instantly understand years of experience, business context, preferences, and judgement. The result is predictable. The AI gives an answer based only on what's in front of it. No history. No context. No understanding of your business. Just the information you've given it in that moment.

The biggest improvements I've seen from AI haven't come from better prompts. They've come from better context. The moment AI understands who you are, what you do, how you think, and what you're trying to achieve, everything changes. The conversation becomes shorter. The output becomes sharper. The revisions become fewer. The work starts sounding like you.

Most people call this prompting, but I think it's briefing.

The skill isn't writing clever instructions. The skill is clearly explaining what good looks like. That's the same skill required to manage people, agencies, designers, developers, and consultants. AI simply exposes whether you have that skill or not.

When I started building The Outlayer, I wasn't a developer. I wasn't a designer. I had never deployed a website. What surprised me wasn't how capable the tools were. It was how much better the output became when I learned to explain exactly what I wanted - not once, but repeatedly. The same way I'd brief an agency, review the work, and give feedback. The tools improved because the context improved.

The future isn't people writing increasingly clever prompts. It's people building systems with enough context to understand their business, their customers, and how they work. The companies that figure this out won't necessarily have better AI. They'll have better institutional knowledge. Better documentation. Better briefs. Clearer thinking.

Because AI doesn't replace clarity, it amplifies it.

Most people don't have a prompt problem; they have a context problem.

That clarity is a big part of what I help founders and operators build now - the context, the briefs, and the systems that make AI actually work for a business instead of against it. If that's what you're wrestling with, reach out.
author: Abhishek Girdhar
meta_title: The Problem Isn't Prompts. It's Context. — The Outlayer
meta_description: I built my company with AI and never wrote a clever prompt. Here's what mattered instead.

title: How to Make AI Remember You - and Stop Re-Explaining Yourself Every Time
excerpt: Set it up like you'd onboard a new hire. Here's how, in Claude and ChatGPT.
content:
Most people talk to AI like a stranger they'll never meet again.

Every conversation starts from zero. You explain who you are, what you do, how you want things written. Then you do it all over again in the next chat.

The work comes back generic, and people blame the tool. The tool is usually fine. It just doesn't know you yet.

The fix is memory - the AI remembering who you are from one conversation to the next. It's built into both Claude and ChatGPT now, and most people already have access to it. The question isn't whether you have it. It's whether you've set it up, or just left it running in the background.

Here's how I set mine up, and how you can do the same in an afternoon.

In this article

  • Brief It Like a New Hire

  • Where Your Context Lives

  • How to Set Up Memory in ChatGPT

  • How to Set Up Memory in Claude

  • What to Put Into Memory

  • Example Memory Setup

  • Keep It Updated

  • Is It Safe?

Brief It Like a New Hire

When someone joins your team, you don't expect them to understand your business on day one. You brief them - what the company does, who the customers are, what good work looks like, what to avoid. Over the first few weeks, they get things wrong, you correct them, and the work improves.

Setting up AI memory is that brief, done once. The more honest and specific it is, the less fixing you'll do later.

Where Your Context Lives

Most people only use one layer of context. The best results come from using all three.

Permanent Memory

This is your always-on brief - who you are, what you do, how you write, who you serve. Anything that's true in almost every conversation.

Projects

Projects are separate workspaces with their own instructions and memory. I keep different projects for content, consulting, and product work. Each one opens with its own context, examples, and objectives, so I don't have to start from scratch every time.

The Conversation

This is the temporary layer, useful for one-off instructions. But if you're repeating the same guidance every week, it belongs in memory or a project instead.

How to Set Up Memory in ChatGPT

  1. Open Settings → Personalization and make sure Memory is turned on.

  2. Open Customize ChatGPT from your profile menu.

  3. Fill in both sections:

    • What ChatGPT should know about you

    • How you'd like ChatGPT to respond

  4. Add new information anytime by saying:

    • "Remember that I..."

    • "Going forward..."

    • "For future conversations..."

  5. For ongoing work, create Projects and add instructions specific to that project.

  6. Review, edit, or delete memories under Settings → Personalization → Memory.

How to Set Up Memory in Claude

  1. Open Settings → Capabilities → Memory and make sure it's enabled.

  2. Create separate Projects for different types of work.

  3. Add instructions inside each Project.

  4. Upload supporting material:

    • Writing samples

    • Brand guidelines

    • Notes

    • Frameworks

    • Reference documents

  5. Work inside the Project instead of starting fresh chats every time.

  6. Review memory at any time in Settings, or simply ask, "What do you remember about me?"

What to Put Into Memory

When I set mine up, I treated it the same way I'd brief a senior hire. I included:

  • Who I am and what I've done

  • What my business does

  • Who I work with

  • My positioning

  • How I write

  • Examples of my work

  • Phrases I use often

  • Phrases I never want it to use

  • Things I care about in the final output

Most people tell AI what to create. Very few tell it how they think. That's where the biggest improvements come from.

Example Memory Setup

Here's a simplified example:

  • I'm a fractional CMO and marketing consultant.

  • I write in plain English.

  • I prefer short paragraphs and direct language.

  • Avoid buzzwords and corporate jargon.

  • Prioritise practical advice over theory.

  • Challenge weak assumptions when appropriate.

  • Use real-world business examples.

  • Don't write like a marketing agency.

That's enough context to dramatically improve the quality and consistency of future conversations.

Keep It Updated

Businesses change. Positioning evolves. Projects end. Priorities shift. If AI is working from an outdated brief, the output will show it.

Every few weeks, ask: "What do you remember about me?" Read the answer. Delete what's outdated. Update what's changed. Five minutes of maintenance can save hours of rework.

Is It Safe?

Don't put anything into memory that you wouldn't want stored. Both Claude and ChatGPT let you review, edit, delete, or temporarily disable memory whenever you want. You stay in control of what gets remembered.

Set It Once

This isn't a big project. An afternoon of honest setup, followed by a few minutes of maintenance now and then. The payoff compounds every conversation after that.

Most people think they need better prompts. In reality, they need a better brief.

That's part of what I help founders and operators get right - the briefs, projects, and systems that make AI actually useful inside a business instead of against it. If that's where you're stuck, reach out.
author: Abhishek Girdhar
meta_title: How to Make AI Remember You - and Stop Re-Explaining Yourself Every Time
meta_description: Set it up like you'd onboard a new hire. Here's how, in Claude and ChatGPT.

title: I had the ideas. I just didn't know where to start.
excerpt: Four days, zero code, no team. How I built and launched my brand with AI.
content:
After I left the agency world, I had too many ideas and no idea where to begin. I knew marketing. I knew how to scale a business and manage people. I had products I wanted to launch. But every idea collided with the next, and I couldn't decide which one to start with. So I waited, for months. On the slide.

The biggest challenge was never the work. It was where to start.

Everything I wanted to build needed people. I've spent years leading teams - when I needed something designed, I went to a designer; something technical, a developer. I always knew what I wanted and how to get it done. I just hadn't done it with my own hands in a long time.

I knew AI could do most of it. I just had to tell the tools clearly what I wanted, and know which tool to use for what. I'd never really used AI before, and I wasn't confident I could pull it off. But I decided to try. The worst case was that I'd fail, and I could live with that.

So I tried. And the first time, I failed, not because the tools couldn't do it, but because I didn't know the right way to use them. The right way comes down to one thing: putting into words exactly what you want. That's the whole skill.

Once I learned that, it moved fast. I built my own brand, turned it into a design system, and then a full website with a CMS and features I wanted. Four days, start to finish. Along the way, I logged into GitHub for the first time in my life - fourteen years of working, and I'd never touched it. I'm an MBA. I've never written a single line of code. But there it was. Live!

Here's what I take from it. I didn't become a creative strategist, developer, or designer. I became someone who started. The tools were always there, available to anyone. The only thing missing was the first step.

This is the work I do now - helping founders and operators use AI to build and scale, instead of waiting.

If you're thinking about an idea but not sure where to start, that was me. The first step is smaller than you think. And if you're sitting on that idea right now, reach out - I'm happy to help you take the first step.
author: Abhishek Girdhar
meta_title: I had the ideas. I just didn't know where to start.
meta_description: Four days, zero code, no team. How I built and launched my brand with AI.

title: How I built a finance education platform from idea to launch in under a week
excerpt: This looks like a story about building a website with AI. But there is a lot more to it than giving prompts to a tool.
content:
Because the order in which it is built is the order that actually decides the outcome. Audience first. Positioning second. Brand third. Only then came design, development, and launch. The planning is the most important part for the rest of it to fall right. Get that sequence wrong and even the cleanest execution ends up solving the wrong problem.

No doubt, AI has dramatically reduced the effort required to build products. But deciding what to build, who it is for, and why anyone should care still matters more than anything. That part is still the work, and that's what I want to walk through.

Here's how it went, step by step:

Step 1: Decide the audience

Before anything else, the question was who this was for and what their real problem was.

The answer was specific. There are a lot of people, especially the young audience in college and the ones who have just started working, who want to understand money but keep getting sold to instead of being educated. The education is missing. Everyone else in the room is trying to close them.

Everything downstream was built to serve that audience and solve that gap.

Step 2: Lock the vision and positioning

Once the audience was clear, the positioning became obvious. This would be an independent financial education platform that never sells anything. No products pushed, and no advice disguised as content. Just helping people understand how money and the markets work.

That single principle, educate and never pitch, became the constraint every decision had to follow.

Positioning is the foundation you build on from the beginning.

Step 3: Build the brain before building the product

This is where Claude came in.

I created a dedicated project inside Claude and used it as the brain for the entire build. I documented everything about Tarun, his background, experience, communication style, audience, goals, and just as importantly, what he should never say.

For a finance educator, those boundaries matter. There is a clear difference between educating and advising, and that distinction had to remain consistent across every page.

I treated those instructions like a brand playbook. Every rule, every guardrail, and every piece of context lived in one place before a single page was created.

This is the step most people skip, and it quietly determines the quality of everything that follows. The quality of what Claude gives you back is usually a reflection of the quality of the context you provide.

Step 4: Build the brand

Next came the brand.

We explored different directions. Should it be built around Tarun's personal name, or should it become an independent brand?

We kept coming back to one idea. The value wasn't a company. The value was a real person teaching from experience without an agenda.

That's how Finance with Tarun came to life.

From there, I used Claude Design to explore multiple visual directions before finalizing the logo, colours, typography, and basic brand guidelines.

Step 5: Design the experience

With the brand in place, I moved to the product interface.

Using Claude Design, I designed every page while thinking about how a real visitor would navigate the platform.

Navigation, usability, SEO, and content hierarchy were considered from the beginning instead of becoming things to fix later.

The objective wasn't simply to make the platform look good. It had to be intuitive, discoverable, and easy to grow over time.

Step 6: Turn the design into a working platform

Once the design was finalized, I used Claude to generate detailed development prompts and then moved into Claude Code to build the platform.

One thing I was firm about from the beginning was the CMS.

I didn't want Tarun to depend on a developer every time he wanted to publish a new article or update the platform. We built a custom CMS, ran everything locally, tested it thoroughly, and only then deployed it.

One thing this project reinforced is that AI rarely struggles with execution. It struggles with ambiguity.

The more precisely you know what you want, the better the output becomes.

The skill that used to be execution is increasingly becoming clarity.

Step 7: Launch

The final stage was deployment.

GitHub handled version control, Supabase became the database, and Vercel handled hosting. The domain was managed through Hostinger and pointed to Vercel.

Within a few days, the platform was live.

The result

The platform is now live at financewithtarun.com.

[Insert screenshots of the homepage, calculators, articles, and key pages]

It scores green across both mobile and desktop on Google PageSpeed Insights, and the content is structured so both traditional search engines and AI-powered search can understand it well.

[Insert Google PageSpeed and AI search screenshots]

The goal was never just to launch quickly. It was to build something that performs well today and remains easy to grow tomorrow.

Final thoughts

This article explains the framework and the sequence I followed.

What it can't fully capture is the judgement behind each decision, whether it's about choosing the right audience, refining the positioning, knowing what and how to build, and recognizing when an output needs another iteration.

AI has made execution dramatically faster.

Experience still shapes the outcome.

If you're sitting on an idea and need someone who can take it from audience and positioning all the way to a working product, that's exactly the kind of work I enjoy doing.
author: Abhishek Girdhar
meta_title: How I built a finance education platform from idea to launch in under a week
meta_description: This looks like a story about building a website with AI. But there is a lot more to it than giving prompts to a tool.

title: Strategy that gets built
excerpt: What changes when the person who draws the plan is also the one who ships it.
content:
Most strategy is sound. That's the part people miss. The thinking holds up, the slides are clear, the room nods — and then the week begins, and the plan stays exactly where it was. On the slide.

A good strategy is a small set of honest choices: where to play, how to win, what to leave alone. Those choices are worth a lot. But they only start paying once they touch something real — a page, a product, a first move a customer can feel.

The deck is not the work

Between the decision and the doing sits a quiet handoff. Someone thinks; someone else builds. The thinking gets written down, passed along, interpreted — and a little of it goes missing at every step.

A plan you can act on Monday beats a perfect one you frame on a wall.

One hand on both

The gap closes when the same person holds both ends — the thinking and the building. When the strategist is also the one who ships, the choices stay intact, because there's no translation step to lose them in.

Build it small, build it now

The fastest way to know a strategy is true is to ship a small version of it. Not the whole plan — the first real move.

What stays after

The point of the work isn't a launch. It's a system that keeps moving once the engagement ends.
author: Abhishek Girdhar

title: The constraint one level down
